note_data.title: Electromagnetic induction
keyPoints: A magnet has a north pole and south pole - like poles experience repulsion, opposite poles experience attraction
Magnetic materials (iron, steel, cobalt, nickel) are attracted to magnets, while non-magnetic materials (glass, plastic) are not affected
Magnetic materials can be magnetised by induced magnetism through stroking with a magnet, hammering in a magnetic field, or placing in a coil with direct current
Temporary magnets lose magnetism when the magnetic field is removed and can be turned on/off, while permanent magnets retain their magnetism
Electromagnets are temporary magnets made by placing soft iron in coiled wire with direct current running through it
Magnetic fields can be visualised using plotting compass (drawing dotted lines following compass direction) or iron filings (sprinkling on paper over magnet)
Electric charge is measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative - opposite charges attract, like charges repel
Conductors allow electric charge to flow through free-moving charged particles, while insulators impede charge flow
Insulators can be electrostatically charged by friction through rubbing two insulators together, transferring electrons from one to the other

definitions.definition: A material or object that produces a magnetic field
A region in which a magnetic pole experiences the force from another magnet or magnetic material
A common temporary magnet made when soft iron is placed within coiled wire with a direct current running through it
A property measured in coulombs that can be positive or negative
The region in which an electric charge experiences attractive/repulsive forces
Electric charge at rest on the surface of insulators when they are charged

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events or detailed account of the process

modelAnswer.question: Explain why transformers are used in the electrical power transmission system from Tarbela Dam to Karachi. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Step-up transformers increase voltage for long-distance transmission. High voltage reduces current in transmission lines. Lower current reduces power losses (as heat) in the cables, making transmission more efficient.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Lower current reduces power losses/heat losses

commonMistakes: Confusing the force between magnet and magnetic material (always attractive) with force between magnetic poles (depends on which poles face each other)
Thinking permanent magnets can be turned on and off like temporary magnets
Forgetting that magnetic field lines must point from north to south pole with arrows showing direction
Assuming all materials are affected by magnets - non-magnetic materials experience no force
examinerTips: Remember that magnetic field strength is shown by how close field lines are together - closer lines indicate stronger field
When drawing magnetic field diagrams, always include arrows on field lines pointing from north to south pole to show direction
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Transformer diagram showing iron core with primary coil (fewer turns) connected to AC supply and secondary coil (more turns) connected to load, demonstrating step-up configuration
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to state Lenz's law and explain how it demonstrates conservation of energy
O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question asking what is required for electromagnetic induction to occur in a wire
M/J 2024 P2 Q6 (5 marks) — students had to explain why an induced e.m.f. is produced when a magnet moves through a coil and calculate the induced current
O/N 2023 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — tested understanding of when electromagnetic induction occurs in a wire with changing conditions

workedExample.question: A rectangular coil of 200 turns with area 0.04 m² is placed in a uniform magnetic field of flux density 0.15 T. The coil is rotated so that the angle between the field and the normal to the coil changes from 0° to 90° in 0.25 s. Calculate the average induced e.m.f. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate initial flux: Φ₁ = BAN cos(0°) = 0.15 × 0.04 × 1 = 0.006 Wb
Step 2: Calculate final flux: Φ₂ = BAN cos(90°) = 0.15 × 0.04 × 0 = 0 Wb
Step 3: Find change in flux: ΔΦ = Φ₂ - Φ₁ = 0 - 0.006 = -0.006 Wb
Step 4: Apply Faraday's law: ε = -N(ΔΦ/Δt) = -200 × (-0.006/0.25) = 4.8 V
workedExample.answer: The average induced e.m.f. is 4.8 V
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct initial flux calculation, 1 mark for final flux = 0, 1 mark for using Faraday's law formula correctly, 1 mark for final numerical answer

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused magnetic flux with magnetic flux density' — always use Φ = BAN cos θ for flux, not just B
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often forgot to include the number of turns when calculating induced e.m.f.' — always multiply by N in Faraday's law
Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates struggled to explain Lenz's law correctly' — always state that induced e.m.f. opposes the CHANGE that caused it, not the original field
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a solenoid with a bar magnet moving through it, showing: (1) magnet with clearly labeled N and S poles, (2) direction of motion with arrow, (3) coil connected to galvanometer or voltmeter, (4) induced current direction using arrows, (5) magnetic field lines around the magnet. Alternative diagram: rectangular coil rotating in uniform magnetic field between two poles, showing angle θ between field lines and normal to coil.
note_data.quickRecap: Magnets have north and south poles with like poles repelling and opposite poles attracting. Magnetic materials can be magnetised temporarily or permanently, with electromagnets being common temporary magnets using soft iron in current-carrying coils. Electric charges measured in coulombs follow similar attraction/repulsion rules, with conductors allowing charge flow and insulators impeding it.
